To pace yourself
I love riding through the mountains.
I tend to avoid busy, paved roads.
What I enjoy most is being alone on the road.
I find it hard to explain the feeling.
You look like you have been riding all day. I'm Fiona, from Manchester. Are you doing the mountain route too?
I am! I'm Marcelo, from Brazil. I love riding off-road, so I always take the scenic route, even when it's a steep climb.
Same here. Most people avoid the hard climbs, but I think they're the best part. So what makes you keep doing it?
The solitude, mostly. And the feeling when you finish something demanding. Reaching the top is the most rewarding thing I know.
I know exactly what you mean. How do you handle the really long days, though?
You learn to pace yourself. I stopped rushing to arrive, and I started enjoying the ride. Honestly, it's exhilarating.
That's beautiful. It's so hard to explain this to people who don't ride, isn't it?
Impossible! I always just say, 'You had to be there.' But honestly, I wouldn't trade any of it for anything.
I love riding off-road and taking the scenic route.
Reaching the top is the most rewarding feeling I know.
I've learned to pace myself and enjoy the ride.
It's hard to put into words. You had to be there.
I wouldn't trade it for anything.

Hi Marcelo, it's Fiona, from the hostel in Tirana. What a night. I still can't stop thinking about your story of riding through Yemen. Most people avoid talking about the hard parts, but you made the suffering sound almost beautiful. Listen, a few of us are planning an off-road trip through the Albanian Alps next spring, lots of steep climbs and rough terrain, exactly the kind of thing you love. I know it is demanding, but the views are worth every drop of sweat. I would really love for you to join us. Think about it. And remember what you said: you don't ride to arrive, you ride to feel alive. Take care, and keep pedaling.

Welcome back to the adventure show. My guest today has cycled across four continents, and I asked him the question everyone asks: why do you do it? His answer stayed with me. He said the thrill is not the point. The point is the solitude, the silence, and the strange freedom of being completely alone with the road. He told me that finishing a brutal climb is the most rewarding feeling he knows, harder than any success at work. And here is the part I love: he said adventure is not about being brave. It is about learning to pace yourself, to keep going when your body wants to stop, and to enjoy the journey instead of rushing to the end. That, he said, is a lesson for life, not just for cycling.
